title: Redis
objectives: Understand Redis via a real-life analogy
Master the core data types: strings, hashes, lists, sets, sorted sets, streams
Know when to use Redis (cache, rate limit, session, queue, pub/sub, leaderboard)
Handle persistence, replication, and clustering correctly

sections.markdown:
**Think of Redis as your kitchen counter next to a distant pantry.**

- The **pantry (your database — Postgres, MySQL)** stores EVERYTHING. Reliable. Huge. Every request has to walk to the pantry, dig through shelves, and walk back. Fine when you cook once. Painful when you cook 50 meals per second.
- The **kitchen counter (Redis)** sits RIGHT NEXT TO YOU. You put the most-used ingredients — salt, pepper, olive oil — on the counter. Next time you cook, you grab from the counter. Instant. Only if the counter's empty do you walk to the pantry.
- The counter is SMALLER than the pantry, so you can't put EVERYTHING there. You keep the HOT stuff (most-accessed, most-recent) and let cold stuff live in the pantry.

**In code:** Redis is an in-memory data store. Reads + writes take **~0.1ms** (vs 5-50ms for Postgres). But it's memory-bound — smaller capacity than disk-based DBs. Used for **caching** (the most common use), **rate limiting**, **session storage**, **pub/sub**, **queues**, **leaderboards**, **distributed locks**. Every serious backend has Redis somewhere.

**Beyond caching — Redis is a Swiss army knife.** It's not just 'faster than Postgres for get/set'. Its native data structures (sorted sets, streams, HyperLogLog) enable use cases that would be awkward in SQL: leaderboards, sliding-window rate limiters, activity feeds, real-time analytics. Learning Redis's data types unlocks entire architectural patterns.

**Redis** (REmote DIctionary Server) is an open-source in-memory data store used as a cache, message broker, and lightweight database. Single-threaded (per command) but blazing fast (~0.1ms per operation). Supports **rich data types** beyond strings: hashes, lists, sets, sorted sets, streams, HyperLogLog, geospatial. Optional persistence (RDB snapshots + AOF log). Supports replication, clustering, pub/sub. Created by Salvatore Sanfilippo (2009); acquired by Redis Ltd; the open-source project rebranded / forked as Valkey in 2024 after license changes.
## The seven core data types — pick the right one

rows: Objects with fields — cheaper than JSON strings

sections.markdown: **The insight that matters:** Redis is fast because it's IN-MEMORY, single-threaded, and its data types are IMPLEMENTED as C data structures (not serialized JSON). A sorted set is a real skip-list + hash map. Operations like `ZRANGEBYSCORE` are O(log N + M) — you can't beat that in an app-layer implementation over Postgres.
## Sample program — Redis as a Spring Boot cache + rate limiter
## Persistence — RDB vs AOF

rows: Fork and dump memory to disk every N seconds
Fast + small file; can lose N seconds on crash
Every write appended to a log
Nearly no data loss; larger file; slower rewrites
Pure cache; lose everything on restart

sections.markdown: **The interview answer:** if Redis is a CACHE, no persistence needed — data reloads from Postgres. If Redis is a PRIMARY store (rate limits, sessions, queues you don't want to lose), enable AOF with `appendfsync everysec` — best balance of durability and throughput.
## Common mistakes
**Mistake 1: No TTL on cache entries.** Redis fills up until it hits `maxmemory`, then evicts (or errors). Every cached entry should have a TTL — `SET key value EX 3600`.

**Mistake 2: Cache stampede.** Cache expires; 1000 concurrent requests all miss + hit DB simultaneously → DB overloads. Fix: singleflight (one request rebuilds; others wait) or probabilistic early expiration.
**Mistake 3: `KEYS *` in production.** Blocks Redis (single-threaded!) for the whole scan. Use `SCAN` (cursor-based, non-blocking) instead.
**Mistake 4: Storing huge blobs.** 100MB values eat memory + block the network on GET. Chunk large data; consider S3 for big blobs.
**Mistake 5: Serialization overhead.** JSON-serializing everything is heavy. Consider MessagePack or plain Redis Hashes for structured data.
**Mistake 6: No `maxmemory` policy.** Redis grows until it OOMs the container. Set `maxmemory` + `maxmemory-policy allkeys-lru` (or another eviction policy).

**Mistake 7: Distributed lock without token + Lua release.** Client A's lock expires → Client B acquires → Client A finishes and `DEL`s → releases B's lock. Fix: unique token + atomic check-and-delete.
## Best practices
**1. Every cache entry has a TTL.** Even 'permanent' values should expire eventually.
**2. `maxmemory` + eviction policy.** `allkeys-lru` for pure cache; `volatile-lru` if you mix cache + non-cache in one DB.
**3. Prefer Hashes for structured objects.** Cheaper than JSON strings; supports partial updates (`HSET`).
**4. Use SCAN instead of KEYS.** Cursor-based; doesn't block Redis.
**5. Distributed lock with token + Lua release.** Prevents accidental release of someone else's lock (Redlock pattern for stronger guarantees).
**6. Persistence matched to use case.** Cache → none. Rate limits + sessions → AOF everysec. Primary store → AOF always + replicas.
**7. Redis Cluster for horizontal scaling.** Shards keys across N masters; each has a replica. Slots are auto-managed.
**8. Monitor slow log.** `SLOWLOG GET 10` catches expensive queries (blocking O(N) commands, hot keys).
## Practice programs
**Practice 1 (Easy) — CLI tour.** `redis-cli`; try SET/GET/INCR, HSET/HGETALL, LPUSH/RPOP, SADD/SMEMBERS, ZADD/ZRANGE.
**Practice 2 (Medium) — Cache a Spring Boot service.** `@Cacheable` on `getUser`; measure latency with/without cache.
**Practice 3 (Medium) — Sliding-window rate limiter.** Build the sample; verify 6th request in a 60s window is rejected.
**Practice 4 (Hard) — Leaderboard.** ZADD scores; ZRANGE for top 10 + ZRANK for user's rank.
**Practice 5 (Hard) — Distributed lock with token + Lua release.** Simulate two clients competing; verify only one enters the critical section.
**Practice 6 (Expert) — Stream-based event log.** XADD orders + XREAD consumer group; simulate multiple workers reading in parallel.
## Interview Q&A — with full answers you can quote

**Q1: What is Redis and why is it so fast?**

**A:** Redis is an in-memory key-value data store that supports rich data types (strings, hashes, lists, sets, sorted sets, streams, HyperLogLog). **Why fast:** (1) **In-memory** — no disk I/O per operation. (2) **Single-threaded** (per command) — no locks, no context switches. (3) **Native C data structures** — a sorted set is a real skip-list + hash map, not a serialized JSON blob. (4) **Simple protocol (RESP)** — binary-safe, easy to parse. Sub-millisecond latency (~0.1ms) for most operations. **Trade-off:** memory-bound; smaller working set than disk-based DBs. **Use cases:** cache (most common), rate limiting, sessions, pub/sub, queues, leaderboards, distributed locks. Every serious backend has Redis somewhere.

**Q2: When would you use a Hash vs a JSON string?**

**A:** **Hash** — a Redis object type with named fields. `HSET user:42 name Ada age 34 email ada@x.com`. **Advantages:** memory-efficient for small objects (special encoding for <100 fields), supports **partial reads** (`HGET user:42 name` — no need to deserialize the whole object), supports **partial writes** (`HSET user:42 email new@x.com` without rewriting everything). **JSON string** — `SET user:42 '{"name":"Ada","age":34,...}'`. Simpler, works with any language, but every read/write is the FULL object. **Rule:** for small structured objects (User, Product, Order), prefer Hash. For deeply nested or type-heterogeneous data, JSON string. Redis JSON module (`RedisJSON`) blends both — first-class JSON with partial ops.

**Q3: What is a cache stampede and how do you prevent it?**

**A:** **Cache stampede** (aka thundering herd): a popular cache entry expires; hundreds of concurrent requests all MISS simultaneously; all hit the underlying DB; the DB drowns. **Prevention:** **(1) Singleflight** — the first request that misses acquires a mutex, rebuilds the cache, and returns. Concurrent misses WAIT for the rebuilder to finish. Most languages have libraries: Java `Caffeine` supports this; Go `singleflight` package. **(2) Probabilistic early expiration** — refresh the cache slightly BEFORE it actually expires, based on a random probability that grows near expiry. Prevents synchronized misses. **(3) Cache warming** — pre-populate before deploy. **(4) Longer TTL + explicit invalidation on writes.** **The right answer** is usually singleflight + short TTL. Every serious cache library supports it out of the box.

**Q4: How do you implement a rate limiter with Redis?**

**A:** Multiple algorithms, all fit Redis: **(1) Fixed window** — one counter per (user, minute). `INCR key EX 60`. Fast but bursty at the window boundary. **(2) Sliding window log** — a sorted set of request timestamps. `ZADD key now now`; `ZREMRANGEBYSCORE key 0 (now - windowMillis)`; `ZCARD key`. Accurate but O(log N + M) per request. **(3) Sliding window counter** — combine two fixed windows with weighted averaging. Smoother than fixed, cheaper than log. **(4) Token bucket** — a Lua script that maintains {tokens, lastRefill}; refills on each request based on elapsed time. Very flexible; used by Kong, envoy, cloud gateways. **Rule:** for simple per-user rate limits, sliding window log is a good default. For high-throughput API gateways, token bucket via a Lua script minimizes round trips. Always test with realistic load.

**Q5: How does Redis persistence work?**

**A:** Two mechanisms: **(1) RDB (Redis Database Backup)** — periodic snapshots. Redis forks; the child writes the entire dataset to a compact binary file. Fast to start, small file. **Trade-off:** you can lose N seconds of data (whatever's between snapshots). **(2) AOF (Append-Only File)** — every write command appended to a log. **`appendfsync always`** — flush on every write (safest, slowest). **`appendfsync everysec`** — flush once/sec (good balance; up to 1s data loss). **`appendfsync no`** — let the OS decide (fast, unpredictable). **Both together** = best durability. **Rule:** cache → no persistence. Rate limiter / session store / queue → AOF everysec. Primary datastore → AOF always + replication + backups. Restart replay: RDB first (fast), then AOF (catches up recent writes).

**Q6: What's Redis Cluster and when do you need it?**

**A:** **Redis Cluster** shards a key space across multiple Redis masters. 16,384 hash slots are distributed across nodes; every key hashes to a slot. Clients can connect to any node — it either serves the request or redirects (MOVED) to the right one. Each master has one or more replicas for high availability. **When:** single Redis instance has ~50-100GB memory ceiling (bigger causes fork stalls during RDB); you need horizontal scaling. **Trade-offs:** **(1)** Multi-key operations only work if keys hash to the same slot — use **hash tags** (`{userId}:profile`, `{userId}:orders`) to group. **(2)** Some commands (MULTI-KEY MSET across slots) don't work across nodes. **(3)** Rebalancing (adding nodes) is manual. **Rule:** below 50GB, run a single primary + 1-2 replicas + Sentinel for HA. Above, Redis Cluster (or Redis Enterprise / Elasticache / MemoryDB).

**Q7: How do distributed locks with Redis work?**

**A:** Simple form: `SET lock:resource <randomToken> NX EX 30`. **NX** = only set if not exists (mutex); **EX 30** = expire after 30s (self-heals if client crashes). Client checks the response — 'OK' means acquired. To release, use a Lua script: `if GET lock:resource == token then DEL lock:resource`. **Why the token?** if client A's lock expires before it finishes (long GC), client B acquires. A then tries to `DEL lock:resource` and releases B's lock — data corruption. The token check makes release safe. **Redlock** — Salvatore's algorithm for multi-node distributed locks; more resilient to individual node failures but more complex and controversial (Martin Kleppmann's critique). **Rule:** for simple in-process serialization, single-node SET NX + Lua release. For genuinely-distributed locks with strong requirements, evaluate ZooKeeper / etcd / a proper consensus system.

## Real-world usage

- **Every serious backend caches with Redis** — Instagram, Twitter, GitHub, Stack Overflow.
- **Rate limiting** — Cloudflare, Kong, envoy, API gateways.
- **Session stores** — Spring Session Redis, Django cache, Rails.
- **Job queues** — Sidekiq (Ruby), Bull (Node), RQ (Python).
- **Pub/sub for real-time** — chat, notifications, live updates.
- **Leaderboards** — gaming, social feeds, ranking systems.
- **Feature flags + config** — LaunchDarkly, Unleash back-ends.
- **Managed services** — AWS ElastiCache, GCP MemoryStore, Azure Cache for Redis, Redis Cloud, Upstash.
- **Valkey** (2024 fork) after Redis's license change — same protocol, permissive license.

## Advanced notes

- **Redis Sentinel** — monitors + auto-failover for master/replica setups.
- **Redis Streams** — Kafka-lite log with consumer groups; simpler for lower volume.
- **RediSearch** — full-text search module.
- **RedisJSON** — first-class JSON with partial ops.
- **RedisBloom** — probabilistic data structures (Bloom filters, Count-Min sketch).
- **RedisGraph** — property-graph queries.
- **RedisTimeSeries** — time-series data.
- **Lua scripting** — atomic multi-command operations; runs inside Redis (`EVAL`).
- **PIPELINE** — batch multiple commands in one round-trip.
- **Client-side caching (RESP3)** — Redis notifies clients of invalidation.
- **`WAIT` command** — synchronous replication wait for stronger durability.
- **CLUSTER commands** — CLUSTER NODES, CLUSTER SLOTS, MIGRATE.
- **Common eviction policies** — `noeviction`, `allkeys-lru`, `volatile-lru`, `allkeys-lfu`, `volatile-random`, `volatile-ttl`.
- **RESP protocol** — client wire format; simple + binary-safe.

flashcards.back: In-memory + single-threaded + native C data structures + simple RESP protocol. ~0.1ms per op.
String, Hash, List, Set, Sorted Set (ZSET), Stream, HyperLogLog.
Singleflight (one request rebuilds, others wait) + probabilistic early expiration.
Fixed window, sliding window log (ZADD + ZREMRANGEBYSCORE + ZCARD), sliding window counter, token bucket.
SET key token NX EX 30 + Lua release with token check.
RDB (snapshots, faster, ~N-second loss) vs AOF (append log, ~1s loss with everysec). Both = best durability.
Above ~50GB memory or when you need to shard write throughput.

options: Singleflight — one request rebuilds; others wait
Lua script that only deletes if token matches
